title: Mitigating Algorithmic Bias
concept: Bias mitigation requires sourcing diverse datasets, applying fairness constraints, and auditing models on split demographics.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can math algorithms still be biased?

options.text: Because they learn directly from imbalanced or biased input data
Because math formulas automatically favor specific skin tones
Because INT8 conversion deletes female face weights

exercises.explain: Neural networks mimic the statistical distributions of their inputs. Skewed inputs produce biased outputs.

cards.title: Fixing the balance
cards.body: Identifying bias is only the first step. As engineers, it is our responsibility to fix it! By balancing our datasets, using fair loss functions, and auditing our performance across different demographic groups, we can build AI that works fairly for everyone.

cards.title: Bias Mitigation Strategies
cards.body: Bias mitigation involves: Pre-processing (oversampling underrepresented groups), In-processing (using loss functions that penalize unfair predictions), and Post-processing (adjusting decision thresholds per group to ensure equal opportunity).

cards.title: A balanced diet
cards.body: If you only eat pizza, you get sick. You fix it by diversifying your diet (balancing data). If you can't get fresh food, you take vitamins (fairness loss constraints) to ensure your body gets all the nutrients it needs to grow strong and healthy.

cards.title: Auditing for fairness
cards.body: A medical tech firm audits an eye-disease detector. They split validation metrics by patient age and gender, discovering a drop in accuracy for seniors. They purchase diverse senior retinal scans to retrain the model, resolving the gap.

cards.title: Fairness doesn't happen automatically
cards.body: You might think that collecting "more data" in general will solve bias. In reality, if you collect 10,000 more random images, you might just repeat the same demographic ratios, maintaining the imbalance. You must collect targeted, diverse data.

cards.title: Fairness Auditing Pipeline
cards.body: How engineers mitigate bias in development:

left.text:
Measures overall accuracy only
Ignores group performance splits
Hides demographic disparities
High risk of biased release

right.text:
Splits metrics by demographics
Calculates equalized odds
Triggers targeted data sourcing
Ensures equitable performance

exercises.prompt: Which bias mitigation step involves collecting more photos of underrepresented demographic groups before training?
exercises.explain: Balancing datasets before training is a pre-processing mitigation strategy.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Simply checking the overall validation accuracy of your model is sufficient to guarantee it is unbiased.
exercises.explain: False. You must evaluate the model's accuracy on split demographic subsets to check for hidden performance gaps.

exercises.sentence: Evaluating a model's accuracy across specific demographic categories like age or gender is called fairness ___.
exercises.explain: Fairness auditing measures discrepancies in model performance across different groups.

options.text: Using specialized loss functions during training that reward fair predictions across groups
Editing the output labels after the model runs inference
Running the model on edge devices instead of the cloud

exercises.explain: In-processing methods modify the model training details (like loss functions or constraints) to promote fairness.

exercises.prompt: You are auditing a recruitment tool that uses computer vision to evaluate video interviews. You discover the model scores older candidates lower because it misinterprets their facial expressions. What is the most effective engineering solution?

options.text: Source a diverse set of training videos representing older age groups, and retrain the model with fairness loss constraints.
Lower the resolution of all video inputs to hide wrinkles.
Apply post-training quantization to speed up processing.
Only allow candidates under 30 to use the software.

exercises.explain: Targeted data sourcing combined with fairness constraints during training is the correct engineering approach to mitigate age-related algorithmic bias.
